---
name: ddx
description: Operates the DDx toolkit for document-driven development. Covers beads (work items), the queue, executions, agents, harnesses, personas, reviews, spec-id. Use when the user says "do work", "drain the queue", "run the next bead", "execute a bead", "review this", "review with fresh eyes", "fold in this guidance", "review again", "break this down into specs and beads", "make this testable", "check against spec", "what's on the queue", "what's ready", "what's blocking the queue", "create a bead", "file this as work", "run an agent", "dispatch", "use a persona", "how am I doing", "ddx doctor", or mentions any ddx CLI command.
---

# DDx

DDx (Document-Driven Development eXperience) is a CLI platform for
document-driven development. It ships a bead tracker (portable work
items with acceptance criteria), a task-execution boundary (DDx
forwards raw passthrough constraints for harness, provider, model,
and profile while power travels as `MinPower`/`MaxPower` bounds;
Fizeau owns concrete routing, provider/model discovery, aliases,
fuzzy matching, catalog lookups, and transcript/session rendering), a
persona system (bindable AI personalities), a library registry
(plugins with prompts, templates, personas), and git-aware
synchronization. This skill makes any skills-compatible coding agent
(Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Gemini CLI, etc.) understand and operate
the DDx surface correctly.

## How this skill works

The skill body you're reading is an **overview** plus an **intent
router**. The real domain guidance lives in `reference/*.md` files.

**Directive: before responding to any DDx-related request, read the
matching reference file from the router table below. The router is
not optional — your answer must be grounded in the reference file's
guidance, not this overview alone.**

## Vocabulary

Single source of truth for DDx terms. Every reference file uses these
exact definitions.

- **Bead** — a portable work item (task, bug, epic, chore) with
  metadata, dependencies, and acceptance criteria. `ddx bead create`.
- **Queue** — the set of active beads. The *execution-ready queue* is
  `status=open` work whose dependencies are closed and which is not parked by
  cooldown, supersession, or execution-eligibility policy. `ddx bead ready`,
  `ddx bead status`.
- **Lifecycle status** — the persisted bead state: `proposed`, `open`,
  `in_progress`, `blocked`, `closed`, or `cancelled`.
- **Ready** — a bead whose dependencies are all closed and which is
  eligible to be picked up next. `ddx bead ready`.
- **Blocked** — `status=blocked` work paused by a hard external recheckable
  blocker. Ordinary dependency waits are derived queue state, not blocked
  status. `ddx bead blocked`.
- **Proposed** — work awaiting operator decision before autonomous execution.
  Proposed beads are not worker-eligible until accepted, rewritten, split,
  blocked externally, cancelled, or otherwise resolved.
- **Claim** — mark a bead as in-progress by an agent (concurrent-write
  protection). `ddx bead update <id> --claim`.
- **Close** — mark a bead as done, with evidence (session, commit
  SHA). `ddx bead close <id>`. Beads only close on execution outcomes
  `success` or `already_satisfied`.
- **Run** — one task invocation atom. `ddx run` calls Fizeau `Execute`
  once with prompt/config, `MinPower`/`MaxPower` bounds, and optional
  raw passthrough constraints.
- **Try** — one bead attempt in an isolated worktree. `ddx try <id>` wraps
  `ddx run` with bead prompt resolution, evidence capture, and merge/preserve
  finalization.
- **Work** — drain the bead queue. `ddx work` picks ready beads and invokes
  `ddx try`; it owns queue iteration and retry policy.
- **Worker** — a `ddx work` / `ddx try` process scoped to a specific project
  root. Do not call every `ddx work` process a "DDx worker" when multiple
  projects are visible. If the user says "DDx worker" without a qualifier,
  assume they mean the worker for the current/requested project. Say "worker
  for `<project>`" or "DDx-powered worker"; reserve "DDx repo worker" for work
  whose project root is the DDx repository.
- **Execution** — a generic DDx execution run (FEAT-010). Includes execution
  definitions, execution records, and execution evidence under `.ddx/runs/<id>/`.
- **Agent** — an AI coding agent (Claude, Codex, Gemini, etc.)
  invoked via a harness. Not a subagent (harness-specific — see
  below).
- **Harness** — a Fizeau routing concept. DDx may pass `--harness` as
  an operator-supplied constraint, but DDx does not validate, rank, fallback, or
  branch on harness names.
- **Persona** — a Markdown file (YAML frontmatter + body) that
  defines an AI personality. DDx injects the body as a system-prompt
  addendum to `ddx run`. `ddx persona list/show/bind`.
- **Role** — an abstract function (e.g., `code-reviewer`,
  `test-engineer`) a workflow can reference. Projects bind roles to
  personas.
- **Binding** — a project-specific `role: persona` map in
  `.ddx/config.yaml` under `persona_bindings`.
- **Power bounds** — `MinPower` and optional `MaxPower` integers passed to the
  upstream execution service. DDx may raise `MinPower` on eligible retries;
  Fizeau owns concrete route selection within those bounds.
- **Plugin** — a self-contained extension installed to
  `.ddx/plugins/<name>/`. The default `ddx` plugin (personas,
  prompts, patterns, templates) is auto-installed by `ddx init`.
  `ddx install <name>`.
- **Skill** — an agentskills.io-standard directory (SKILL.md +
  optional `reference/`, `evals/`, `scripts/`). This `ddx` skill is
  the one DDx ships. Plugins can ship additional skills.
- **Subagent** — a harness-local concept for running a prompt in an
  isolated context (Claude Code's `.claude/agents/` + `context:
  fork`; Codex's `agents/`; others differ). DDx does not specify subagent
  orchestration; that remains harness business.
- **Update** — refresh plugin/toolkit content to a newer version.
  `ddx update [<plugin>]`.
- **Upgrade** — replace the DDx binary with a newer release.
  `ddx upgrade`.
- **Review** — two distinct concepts. **Bead review**
  (`ddx bead review <id>`) grades a completed bead against its
  acceptance criteria. **Comparison/adversarial review** is a workflow skill
  composition over `ddx run`, not a core quorum flag. See `reference/review.md`.
- **Governing artifact** — the document that authorizes a bead's
  work: a FEAT-\*, SD-\*, TD-\*, or ADR-\* under `docs/`. Referenced
  via `spec-id`.
- **Spec-id** — the `spec-id: <ID>` custom field on a bead pointing
  at its governing artifact.

## Intent router

Before responding, read the matching file.

| User says / asks about | Read this file |
|---|---|
| "what should I work on next", "what's blocking the queue", "review with fresh eyes", "fold in this guidance", "review again", "break this down into specs and beads", "make this testable", broad queue orientation or planning | `reference/interactive.md` |
| write/plan work, "create a bead", "file this as work", bead metadata, acceptance criteria, dependencies | `reference/beads.md` |
| `ddx work`, `ddx try <id>`, "execute bead `<id>`", "drain the queue", "run the next bead", "start the worker", "start N workers and watch", "monitor the drain", verify-and-close | `reference/work.md` |
| "review this", "check against spec", bead review, quorum review, code review, adversarial check | `reference/review.md` |
| "assess bead readiness", "score a bead", "triage a failed attempt", "refine a bead", bead authoring lint | `bead-lifecycle/` |
| "run an agent", "dispatch", harness/provider/model passthrough, power, effort, "use a persona", role bindings | `reference/agents.md` |
| "what's on the queue", "what's ready", "how am I doing", health check, "ddx doctor", sync status | `reference/status.md` |

If the intent spans multiple files (e.g., "create a bead and then
run it"), read beads.md first, then work.md. If no match, ask the
user which concept they mean rather than guessing.

## Top-level policy reminders

These apply across all DDx operations. Do not restate them in every
reference file; do not violate them.

- **Never edit `.ddx/beads.jsonl` directly.** All tracker changes go
  through `ddx bead create/update/close/dep`. Direct edits corrupt
  bead history and cannot be audited.
- **Tracker changes are commit-worthy.** After `ddx bead create`,
  `update`, `dep add/remove`, or `close`, commit the resulting
  `.ddx/beads.jsonl` change — either as a tracker-only commit or
  folded into the same commit as related implementation changes.
- **Preserve bead-attempt commit history.** Branches containing
  `ddx try` / `ddx work` execution commits carry an audit trail. **Never
  squash, rebase, filter, or amend** these commits. Use only
  `git merge --ff-only` or `git merge --no-ff` when merging.
  `gh pr merge --squash` and `--rebase` are forbidden on these
  branches.
- **Work in worktrees for parallel agents.** Use `wt switch -c
  <branch>` (worktrunk) or equivalent to give each concurrent agent
  its own isolated checkout. `ddx try` does this automatically;
  manual parallel work should too.
- **Power-first execution dispatch.** Default to `ddx run`/`ddx try`/`ddx work`
  with power bounds. `--harness`, `--provider`, and `--model` are passthrough
  constraints only; DDx must not use them for routing policy.

## Links out

- Full CLI reference: `ddx --help`, `ddx <subcommand> --help`.
- Governing feature specs: see `FEAT-*` documents under your
  project's `docs/` tree — especially the CLI, beads, agent-service,
  executions, and skills features.
- Personas README: shipped by the default `ddx` plugin at
  `.ddx/plugins/ddx/personas/README.md`.
- Open standard this skill conforms to:
  [agentskills.io](https://agentskills.io).

data.output:
# Work — Drain the Queue, Execute, Verify, Close

## Mode note

This reference covers **`bead_execution` mode**: explicit worker commands such as
`ddx work`, `ddx try <id>`, "execute bead `<id>`", or "start the worker".

If the user said "what should I work on next?", "what's blocking the queue?", or
"work the queue" without an explicit bead ID or `ddx work` command, they are in
**`interactive-steward`** mode — see `reference/interactive.md`. The steward
plans; the worker executes. Do not instruct manual ready-bead implementation in
response to broad orientation prompts.

---

"Doing work" in DDx means draining the ready queue: pick the top ready bead,
run one or more `ddx try` attempts, verify the result, and close the bead on
success or leave it available for a future eligible retry.

## Default surface: `ddx work`

```bash
ddx work
```

`ddx work` drains the queue by picking ready beads and invoking `ddx try`.
`ddx try` wraps `ddx run`, which is the single agent invocation primitive. DDx
owns queue iteration, attempt evidence, and retry policy; Fizeau owns concrete
routing, provider/model discovery, alias resolution, fuzzy matching, catalog
lookups, and transcript/session rendering.

Flags worth knowing:

- `--once` — pick one bead and stop (don't loop).
- `--watch` — keep running after the current ready queue drains; wait for newly-ready beads.
- `--idle-interval <dur>` — sleep duration between empty-queue scans in watch mode (default 30s).
- `--min-power <n>` / `--max-power <n>` — requested agent power bounds.
- `--top-power` — choose a `MinPower` threshold from the service's reported
  power bands.
- `--harness <name>` / `--provider <name>` / `--model <ref>` / `--profile <name>` — passthrough constraints only. DDx sends them
  unchanged and does not route on them.

## Pre-claim Intake and Silent-Idle Diagnosis

The worker's pre-claim intake hook evaluates a `readiness_checks` payload before
claiming a bead. The canonical verdict forms are:

- JSON bool `true` -> `pass`
- JSON bool `false` -> `fail`
- JSON strings are passed through after trimming
- `null` or absent -> empty

`ClassifyReadinessWithMode`
(`cli/internal/agent/readiness_classification.go:56-115`) is the mapping layer
between readiness classifications and worker behavior:

- `system_unready` / `intake_error` are hard errors from the hook, but the
  worker fail-opens and skips the claim instead of parking the bead.
- `needs_refine` is warn-only in warn mode and becomes operator-attention /
  park in block mode.
- `operator_required` parks the bead.
- `needs_split` parks the bead for decomposition.

When the worker appears to idle on a full queue, inspect `loop.idle` events
first. That event stream is the diagnostic surface for a silent stall. Repeated
identical blocker details are escalated after
`preClaimIdleEscalationThreshold` cycles (`ddx-df77e668`), which should convert
the stall into a `loop.operator_attention` signal instead of letting the worker
spin forever. The main blocker codes to distinguish are
`preclaim_systemic` and `preclaim_tracker_contention`.

If the queue is still making claims but the rate is suspiciously low, use the
claim-success-rate warning knobs `--claim-rate-window` and
`--claim-rate-threshold` to surface that condition.

## Are workers running for this project?

When someone asks "is the worker still working beads?" or "how is the queue
progressing?", do not answer with a global `ps aux | grep -E 'ddx work|ddx try'`
scan. That returns workers from every checkout on the host and falsely
signals progress on whichever project happens to be asked about.

```bash
ddx work status                # live workers scoped to this project
ddx work status --json         # machine-readable
ddx work status --all-projects # explicit cross-project escape hatch
```

`ddx work status` defaults to the current project root (CLI flag → env
`DDX_PROJECT_ROOT` → CWD git root). It reports pid, age, command line,
project root, and — when inferable from argv or an isolated execute-bead
worktree — the active bead ID. Always name the project root the answer
applies to; never substitute a worker from a different repository as
evidence that this project is progressing.

When multiple repositories have workers, report them project-first:

```text
<project-root>
  pid=<pid> bead=<bead-id> phase=<phase>
```

Use "worker for `<project-root>`" for project-scoped status. Use
"DDx-powered worker" for a generic `ddx work` process. Use "DDx repo worker"
only when the project root is the DDx repository. If the user says "DDx
worker" without a qualifier, assume the current/requested project and do not
surface workers from other projects.

`--all-projects` is the only sanctioned way to ask "what ddx workers
exist anywhere on this host?" — use it only when the question is
explicitly cross-project.

## Primitive: `ddx try`

For targeted re-runs, debugging, or running a specific bead:

```bash
ddx try <bead-id>
ddx try <bead-id> --from <rev>      # base commit override
ddx try <bead-id> --no-merge        # preserve result, don't land
```

`ddx try` runs one bead attempt in an isolated git worktree. It calls `ddx run`
for the actual agent invocation.

## Pick by default, not by ID

Under normal operation, **don't specify a bead ID**. `ddx work` picks
the top ready bead based on priority + dependency satisfaction. Only
pin a specific ID when debugging (`ddx try <id>`) or
when the queue ordering would pick the wrong bead and you need to
override.

## Starting and monitoring workers in the background

When the user asks you to **start workers and watch progress**, do
not invent your own `tail | grep` pipeline — the obvious shapes have
all bitten in the field. Use these vetted patterns.

### One worker, foreground drain

If the user just wants to drain once and read the result at the end,
run `ddx work` directly and let it print. Don't wrap with `head`,
`tail`, or `tee` — pipes to those tools buffer stdout for the entire
run, so the user sees nothing during execution and everything at the
end.

### One or more workers, watch live

```bash
# Start each worker, one log per worker:
ddx work > /tmp/ddx-worker1.log 2>&1 &   # repeat for worker2, etc.

# Stream with per-worker prefixes (drops the `==>` separator noise
# that `tail -F` injects when switching files):
(tail -F /tmp/ddx-worker1.log 2>/dev/null | awk '{print "w1: "$0; fflush()}' &
 tail -F /tmp/ddx-worker2.log 2>/dev/null | awk '{print "w2: "$0; fflush()}' &
 wait) | grep -E --line-buffered \
  "^w[0-9]+: (▶|✗|✓|→ |worker exited|attempts:|failed:|landing worktree has|lock_contention|panic|FATAL|excluded|caller_excluded|ladder exhausted|escalation|powerClass=|do route|i/o timeout|connection refused|provider error)"
```

Why those specific tokens — together they cover **every actionable
event** in a drain:

- `▶ <bead>` — a new bead was claimed. `✗` and `✓` close it.
- `→` — outcome arrow, also follows a bead-state transition.
- `worker exited` / `attempts:` / `failed:` — end-of-drain summary.
- `landing worktree has` — pre-claim hook hit a stale index; queue
  is jammed if recovery doesn't kick in.
- `lock_contention`, `panic`, `FATAL` — infrastructure problems.
- `excluded`, `caller_excluded`, `ladder exhausted`,
  `escalation`, `powerClass=` — the escalation path actually
  firing; absence on a failed bead means the loop is silently
  no-op'ing instead of climbing the ladder.
- `do route` — the implementation-phase routing decision, surfaces
  the provider/model that's about to be hit. Without this in the
  alternation, the monitor goes quiet during the most interesting
  window (provider call) and only resurfaces on success/failure.
- `i/o timeout`, `connection refused`, `provider error` — transport
  failures. Two in a row to the same `(provider, model)` is the
  signature of routing that isn't honoring exclusions.

### What NOT to do

- **Don't wrap `ddx work` in `| tail -N`** to "see the last N
  lines". The pipe buffers until the producer exits, so you see
  nothing for the whole run. Redirect to a file and tail the file
  separately.
- **Don't grep `^==>` into the alternation.** Without the awk
  prefix, `tail -F file1 file2` emits `==> filename <==` whenever
  the active file switches — every switch becomes a noise event in
  a Monitor pipeline. Strip those, or prefix lines with `awk`.
- **Don't tail without `--line-buffered`/`fflush()`.** Pipe
  buffering means events arrive in 4KB chunks (often minutes apart)
  instead of as they happen. The `awk` form above flushes per line.
- **Don't share one log file across workers.** Interleaved output
  destroys attributability and any chance of debugging which worker
  hit what.

## Verify independently before closing

**Agents hallucinate successful completions.** Do not trust the
agent's self-report. Before closing a bead:

1. Run the acceptance-criteria command yourself (from the bead's
   `accept:` field). If it's `go test ./foo/...`, run that.
2. Check the resulting commit against the in-scope file list from
   the bead description. Out-of-scope files touched? Reject the
   attempt.
3. Read the commit message — does it reference the bead ID?
   (`[ddx-<id>]` or similar in the commit subject is the convention.)

If all three pass: close the bead.

## Close on success, unclaim on failure

DDx try/work outcomes form a specific taxonomy. Each outcome
maps to a concrete follow-up action:

During a live `ddx work` / `ddx try` attempt, the current bead's lifecycle is
orchestrator-owned. The worker executing that bead must not run
`ddx bead update <bead-id> --claim`, `ddx bead update <bead-id> --status <status>`,
`ddx bead update <bead-id> --unclaim`, or `ddx bead close <bead-id>` for the
current bead. The Step 0 split-first path still allows child-bead
`ddx bead create`, `ddx bead dep add`, and the parent-note update
`ddx bead update <parent-id> --notes 'decomposed into <child-ids>'`.

| Outcome | Meaning | Action |
|---|---|---|
| `success` | Tests pass, AC met, commit landed | `ddx bead close <id>` |
| `already_satisfied` | No changes needed (AC was already green) | `ddx bead close <id>` |
| `no_changes` | Agent returned without producing commits and wrote `no_changes_rationale.txt` | Leave open, **unclaim** |
| `no_evidence_produced` | Agent exited without a commit or rationale | Leave open, **unclaim**, investigate harness/commit failure |
| `land_conflict` | Merge conflict on landing the result | Leave open, **unclaim** |
| `post_run_check_failed` | Tests or gate failed after landing | Leave open, **unclaim**, investigate |
| `execution_failed` | Agent subprocess errored (timeout, crash, provider error) | Leave open, **unclaim** |
| `structural_validation_failed` | Result failed structural sanity check | Leave open, **unclaim**, investigate |

`ddx work` applies these actions automatically. If you're running
`ddx try` directly, apply them manually: `ddx bead update <id>
--unclaim` to release a bead after a non-closing outcome, so another
worker can pick it up.

**Never leave a bead half-owned** — every execution either closes or
unclaims.

## Testing expectations for bead implementations

When an agent implements a bead, its output should include tests
that exercise the new code:

- **Unit tests** for logic with in-memory stubs for first-party
  collaborators. Favor stubs over mocks; mocks that assert on call
  sequences test implementation, not behavior.
- **Integration tests** against real collaborators where the cost
  is small (real git in a temp dir, real DB in a temp file, real
  HTTP via a local test server).
- **Real e2e tests** at the outermost boundary. E2e tests that mock
  the database or the network are unit tests lying about their
  scope.

docs/docker-attempt-build-environments.md:47:provider environment variables through and mounts a minimal per-attempt auth
docs/prose-quality.md:5:checkable, and more aligned with DDx vocabulary without rewriting the author's

website/content/features/_index.md:66:Every execution writes a bundle under `.ddx/executions/<timestamp>-<hash>/` with the prompt, the agent transcript, token and cost telemetry, the diff produced, and the merge outcome. Evidence stays in the repository, attached to the commit, so future reviewers (human or model) can reconstruct what happened without rerunning the agent.
website/content/features/_index.md:88:DDx talks to agents through `ddx run`. Swap Claude for Codex for a local model by changing configuration, not commands. The same `ddx run` invocation works across providers; cost, tokens, and latency are recorded the same way regardless of which harness answered.

docs/plans/plan-2026-05-10-mcp-architecture.md:27:- **Doc graph** — `ddx_doc_graph`, `ddx_doc_deps`, `ddx_doc_dependents`, `ddx_doc_stale`, `ddx_doc_show` (`server.go:3360-3413`). "Specs touching auth area" works with a path/label filter on `ddx_doc_graph`.
docs/plans/plan-2026-05-10-mcp-architecture.md:40:- **Routing / auth pass-through** — ts-net auth + project scoping resolved in ddx-server.
docs/plans/plan-2026-05-10-mcp-architecture.md:42:The hybrid model (Axon-MCP + DDx-MCP both exposed) is technically possible but **bad UX for an Assistant**: tool-selection from natural language frequently picks the wrong one, and auth surface doubles. One MCP per Assistant.
docs/plans/plan-2026-05-10-mcp-architecture.md:47:2. **Deployment surface** — no Databricks App packaging exists (no app config, no entry script, no Postgres-via-Axon connection sample). ts-net (ADR-006) is the documented auth path; Databricks Apps will need a non-tsnet listener mode behind the App's auth proxy.
docs/plans/plan-2026-05-10-mcp-architecture.md:60:- The bigger blocker for a Databricks deployment isn't MCP design — it's auth (gap 1), Axon production-readiness (gap 3), and packaging (gap 2).
docs/plans/plan-2026-05-10-mcp-architecture.md:66:- For gap 1 (auth): does ddx-server need its own user model, or can it lean entirely on Databricks workspace OAuth + the App's auth proxy?
library/prompts/common/docs.md:33:- Use lowercase with hyphens: `api-authentication.md`
library/prompts/common/docs.md:47:- Use sentence case for subheadings: `## How authentication works`
docs/plans/plan-2026-05-11-website-autogen.md:12:The parent plan (`plan-2026-05-11-website-evolution.md`) marks auto-generation as the spine: "no hand-authoring, no LLM, no staleness." This document specifies the auto-generation surface AND the templating system together — they interact: generators produce data; templates render it; tokens style it; freshness is enforced in CI.
docs/plans/plan-2026-05-11-website-autogen.md:27:| 2 | **Templates share design tokens via a single CSS file imported by one `baseof.html` chrome.** Tokens never duplicate. |
docs/plans/plan-2026-05-11-website-autogen.md:28:| 3 | **Auto-generation is the spine.** No hand-authoring, no LLM, no staleness. Deterministic generators project the artifact graph into Hugo data files at CI time; generated artifacts are not version-controlled. |
docs/plans/plan-2026-05-11-website-autogen.md:86:      tokens.css           # ALL design tokens (palette, type scale, spacing, breakpoints)
docs/plans/plan-2026-05-11-website-autogen.md:87:      base.css             # reset + element styles, consumes tokens.css
docs/plans/plan-2026-05-11-website-autogen.md:88:      components.css       # nav, card, button, hero, callout, badge — consumes tokens.css
docs/plans/plan-2026-05-11-website-autogen.md:89:      layouts.css          # page-level grids — consumes tokens.css
docs/plans/plan-2026-05-11-website-autogen.md:96:      single.html          # generic single-page (for hand-authored content like /why/)
docs/plans/plan-2026-05-11-website-autogen.md:125:  content/                 # hand-authored only — no auto-gen output here
docs/plans/plan-2026-05-11-website-autogen.md:133:### How tokens propagate without duplication
docs/plans/plan-2026-05-11-website-autogen.md:135:Unchanged from v1. `baseof.html` is the only file that imports CSS via `partial "head.html"`. Every other layout extends `baseof` via `{{ define "main" }}`. Change tokens.css → every page updates. Add a content type → new layout extending baseof inherits chrome automatically.
docs/plans/plan-2026-05-11-website-autogen.md:250:The `artifact-types.yaml` is read by `_default/artifact-list.html` to render section indexes — no hand-authored section metadata anywhere. Adding a new artifact type means a plugin declares it; the website picks it up automatically.
docs/plans/plan-2026-05-11-website-autogen.md:372:| **A1 — Templating foundation** | `layouts/_default/baseof.html`, `head.html`, `nav.html`, `footer.html`, `tokens.css`, `base.css`, `components.css`, `layouts.css`. Update `layouts/index.html` to extend `baseof`. |
docs/plans/plan-2026-05-11-website-autogen.md:415:What stays hand-authored: `content/_index.md` (homepage editorial copy), `content/why/` (narrative content). The plan explicitly acknowledges these as exempt — but identifies opportunities (active FEAT list, current focus, recent activity) that could come from project state in a future iteration.

Concrete cost is small because `BlobStore` is write-once-per-key with caller-supplied keys.

.ddx/beads.jsonl:1:{"acceptance":"1. A reusable delimiter helper exists for untrusted artifact bodies.
2. Prompt assembly uses the helper at every artifact-body interpolation site in scope.
3. `TestArtifactBodyDelimitedAsUntrusted` proves the delimiter contract and payload preservation.
4. `cd cli && go test ./internal/server/... ./internal/agent/...` passes.
5. `lefthook run pre-commit` passes.","created_at":"2026-05-05T11:37:14.708641486Z","description":"PROBLEM
Prompt-injection boundary markers are not yet explicit around artifact bodies that later flow into prompt assembly. The current server-side artifact readers in `cli/internal/server/state_executions.go:205-217` clamp text for transport, but they do not wrap the body in an explicit untrusted-data envelope for downstream prompt renderers. The review prompt path in `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go:1561-1630` only uses XML tags for the bead envelope; it does not mark artifact content with a dedicated boundary contract.

ROOT CAUSE
There is no shared delimiter helper for untrusted artifact bodies. The current prompt builders rely on ad hoc XML wrappers and transport clamping, which are not the same thing as a prompt-injection boundary that clearly distinguishes trusted instructions from untrusted artifact text.

PROPOSED FIX
- Add a small helper (likely in `cli/internal/evidence` or the prompt-rendering package that owns the assembler) that wraps artifact bodies in a canonical untrusted-data delimiter pair.
- Update the prompt-rendering call sites that inline artifact bodies to use the wrapper before interpolation.
- Add a regression test named `TestArtifactBodyDelimitedAsUntrusted` that asserts the delimiter pair appears around the artifact payload and that the raw body is still preserved inside the wrapper.

NON-SCOPE
- Do not alter the transport-level clamp behavior or its `truncated` metadata.
- Do not change review verdict parsing or the FEAT-022 telemetry schema in this slice.
- Do not rewrite unrelated server text egress paths.","events":[{"actor":"ddx","body":"{"resolved_provider":"codex","resolved_model":"gpt-5.4-mini","fallback_chain":[],"actual_power":8}","created_at":"2026-05-05T14:44:37.174355984Z","kind":"routing","source":"ddx agent execute-bead","summary":"provider=codex model=gpt-5.4-mini"},{"actor":"ddx","body":"{"attempt_id":"20260505T143454-88ed54dc","harness":"codex","model":"gpt-5.4-mini","input_tokens":4861400,"output_tokens":17293,"total_tokens":4878693,"cost_usd":0,"duration_ms":580211,"exit_code":0}","created_at":"2026-05-05T14:44:37.425832645Z","kind":"cost","source":"ddx agent execute-bead","summary":"tokens=4878693 model=gpt-5.4-mini"},{"actor":"ddx","body":"{"escalation_count":0,"fallback_chain":[],"final_tier":"","requested_profile":"","requested_tier":"","resolved_model":"gpt-5.4-mini","resolved_provider":"codex","resolved_tier":""}","created_at":"2026-05-05T14:44:44.068530101Z","kind":"routing","source":"ddx agent work","summary":"provider=codex model=gpt-5.4-mini"},{"actor":"erik","body":"failure_class=review-error: transport

.ddx/beads.jsonl:12:{"acceptance":"1. cli/tools/lint/routinglint/ (package routinglint) contains the new rule or an integration test that enforces the Profile/Harness/Model-on-Runtime prohibition for run/try/work paths.
2. TestRoutingLint_RunPathNeverSetsProfileDirectly in cli/tools/lint/routinglint/ (or a new integration test file): runs go run ./tools/lint/routinglint/cmd/routinglint ./... against the live tree and asserts zero violations for the run/try/work pattern.
3. TestViolations (cli/tools/lint/routinglint/analyzer_test.go:12) still passes.
4. TestClean (cli/tools/lint/routinglint/analyzer_test.go:21) still passes.
5. cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1891-1897 CLIOverrides usage verified compliant (CLIOverrides is not a Runtime struct; no lint exemption needed).
6. Any actual violations found in run/work/try paths are fixed or annotated with routinglint:legacy-rejection reason="...".
7. cd cli && go test ./tools/lint/routinglint/... green.
8. lefthook run pre-commit passes.","created_at":"2026-05-02T07:02:50.959795835Z","description":"PROBLEM
  The run/try/work dispatch paths must not set Profile, Harness, or Model directly on ResolvedConfig or Runtime structs — those are durable knobs that belong in config.LoadAndResolve. Without a mechanically-enforced lint rule, future edits can silently reintroduce the anti-pattern and bypass the fizeau routing contract.

ROOT CAUSE
  cli/tools/lint/routinglint/analyzer.go (package routinglint) currently forbids retired compensating-routing identifiers (ResolveProfileLadder, ResolveTierModelRef, etc.) and flag literals (--escalate, profile_ladders). It does not yet forbid direct field assignment of Profile/Harness/Model on Runtime structs inside run/try/work command code paths. The SD-024 runtimelint analyzer (cli/tools/lint/runtimelint/analyzer.go:41) forbids durable-knob fields on *Runtime structs generically, but does not name a per-command allowlist or produce a test that verifies the run/work/try paths specifically. No TestRoutingLint* test exercises this invariant.

PROPOSED FIX
  - Extend routinglint (cli/tools/lint/routinglint/analyzer.go) to flag direct Profile/Harness/Model field assignment on types matching *Runtime in cli/cmd/ run/work/try command files, or add a new rule in runtimelint covering this.
  - Alternatively, add a targeted integration test TestRoutingLint_RunPathNeverSetsProfileDirectly that runs the analyzer against the live tree and asserts zero diagnostics for the assignment pattern.
  - Fix any existing violations: cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1893-1897 passes overrides.Profile/Harness/Model through CLIOverrides to config.LoadAndResolve — verify this is compliant (CLIOverrides is not a Runtime struct).

NON-SCOPE
  - Changes to agent_run or agent_run_overrides command behavior.
  - Changes to the fizeau or ladder routing logic.
  - Lint rules for non-routing fields on Runtime structs (covered by runtimelint).","events":[{"actor":"erik","body":"{"detail":"description drops commitment \"cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1893\"","intake_outcome":"ambiguous_needs_human"}","created_at":"2026-05-08T06:33:09.032244684Z","kind":"intake.blocked","source":"ddx agent work","summary":"ambiguous_needs_human"},{"actor":"ddx","body":"","created_at":"2026-05-08T15:24:50.040104278Z","kind":"lifecycle_reconciled","source":"ddx bead reconcile","summary":"expired retry-after metadata no longer blocks execution"},{"actor":"ddx","body":"from_status=open

.ddx/beads.jsonl:15:{"acceptance":"1. Add `TestExecutionCleanupManager_ApplyReapsOnlyStaleAttemptProcessGroups` using injected scanner/killer fakes where needed, proving `--apply` terminates only stale DDx-owned attempt process groups.
2. Add `TestExecutionCleanupManager_ApplyPreservesLiveAndForeignProcessGroups` proving live run-state, live worker sidecar, active registered worktree, other-project processes, and uncertain candidates are preserved with operator-visible warnings when appropriate.
3. Add `TestExecutionCleanupManager_ApplyTerminatesGracefullyThenBoundedKill` or an equivalent assertion proving the killer uses graceful terminate before bounded kill when a process group remains alive.
4. Existing process-reaper tests remain green: `cd cli && go test ./internal/agent/... -run 'OrphanHarness|Executor|DockerCloneBackend_ProcessGroupCleanup' -count=1` passes.
5. `cd cli && go test ./internal/agent/... -run 'ExecutionCleanupManager_Apply.*Process|ProcessReap|AttemptDescendant|OrphanHarness|Executor|DockerCloneBackend_ProcessGroupCleanup' -count=1` passes.
6. `lefthook run pre-commit` passes.","created_at":"2026-06-28T00:56:11.27602192Z","description":"PROBLEM
`ddx cleanup --apply` can delete stale DDx execution files, but it cannot terminate stale DDx-owned attempt-descendant process groups that are provably orphaned. Heavy leftover processes can continue burning CPU or holding ports after their original attempt is gone.

ROOT CAUSE WITH FILE:LINE
- `cli/internal/agent/execution_cleanup.go:230-527` applies file cleanup for stale temp dirs, worktrees, scratch roots, stale run-state files, evidence dirs, logs, worker dirs, and refs, but contains no safe process-group termination path.
- `cli/internal/agent/execution_cleanup.go:110-138` has no process reaping counts or warnings in `ExecutionCleanupSummary`, so apply mode cannot report terminated, preserved, blocked, or unsafe process groups.
- `cli/internal/agent/orphan_harness_reaper.go:82-100` and `cli/internal/agent/orphan_harness_reaper.go:155-168` intentionally gate startup harness reaping on top-level harness names and dead bead lease owners; that does not cover descendant process groups whose harness root has already exited.
- `cli/internal/agent/executor_unix.go:10-29` sets process group and parent-death attributes for normal harness children, which cleanup can use only when stale ownership and liveness evidence prove the group is DDx-owned and safe to reap.

PROPOSED FIX
Extend execution cleanup apply mode with an injectable process-group killer and conservative stale ownership checks. Apply may terminate only process groups associated with the current project's DDx execution root where run-state cleanup liveness is expired or missing, the recorded owner PID is gone, the worktree is under the current project execution root, and no live worker sidecar or active registered worktree owns it. Preserve live run-state, live worker sidecar, active registered worktree, other-project processes, and any uncertain candidate. Prefer graceful terminate followed by bounded kill where feasible. Surface blocked warnings instead of silently deleting files when a process is detected but cannot be killed safely.

IN-SCOPE FILES
- `cli/internal/agent/execution_cleanup.go` and adjacent new platform-specific process cleanup files/tests.
- `cli/internal/workerstatus/*` or `cli/internal/agent/orphan_harness_reaper*` only for reused process-scanning helpers, not broad rewrites.
- `docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-010-task-execution.md` and `docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-025-task-execution-lifecycle.md` only if this child changes the documented cleanup contract.

NON-SCOPE
- Do not edit Niflheim.
- Do not duplicate `ddx-e0de6369`; that bead owns `.ddx/harness-sessions/` scratch ignore/prune behavior.
- Do not duplicate `ddx-bbea954f`; that bead owns dirty timeout worktree edit preservation before cleanup deletion.
- Do not replace the existing parent-death/process-group fixes from `ddx-0d7d5f0e`, `ddx-3113d524`, or `ddx-418c3849`.
- Do not implement a general-purpose port scanner or host process manager. Port conflict recovery here comes only from safely reaping stale DDx-owned attempt process groups.
- Do not kill processes outside the current project's DDx execution root, processes tied to live run-state/worker liveness, active registered worktrees, or non-DDx user workloads.

GOVERNING ARTIFACTS
- `spec:FEAT-010`
- `spec:SD-025`

PARENT AND DEPS
Parent: `ddx-3106674f` (multi-worker reliability and Niflheim local gates).

.ddx/beads.jsonl:20:{"acceptance":"1. TestManagedWorkerRecordsProcessGroup proves StartExecuteLoop creates a server-managed drain worker with nonzero PID and PGID in WorkerRecord and persisted status.json.
2. TestManagedWorkerServerManagedFlagRunsWorkLoop proves the hidden ddx work --server-managed <worker-id> entry point reads the persisted spec and runs the same execute-loop path used by current server-managed workers.
3. TestManagedWorkerProcessBoundarySkipsExternalReportedWorkers proves process-boundary recording is applied only to server-owned work workers, not external reported workers or interactive Claude/Codex sessions.
4. cd cli && go test ./internal/server/... -run 'TestManagedWorkerRecordsProcessGroup|TestManagedWorkerServerManagedFlagRunsWorkLoop|TestManagedWorkerProcessBoundarySkipsExternalReportedWorkers' passes.

6. lefthook run pre-commit passes.","created_at":"2026-06-27T22:58:08.247681142Z","description":"PROBLEM
Server-managed drain workers are still started as in-process goroutines, so their WorkerRecord usually has PID zero and no PGID. The server cannot reliably target the ddx work process tree that later launches claude, codex, shell, or sleep descendants.

ROOT CAUSE WITH FILE:LINE
cli/internal/server/workers.go:123-127 records only PID and has no persisted PGID. cli/internal/server/workers.go:192 defines WorkerManager as managing in-process work workers as goroutines. cli/internal/server/workers.go:438 creates a cancelable context, and cli/internal/server/workers.go:461-462 starts drainProgress and runWorker goroutines instead of a server-owned OS process. cli/internal/server/workers.go:414-436 writes the initial WorkerRecord before any managed worker process boundary exists.

PROPOSED FIX
Add a server-managed subprocess launch path for StartExecuteLoop in cli/internal/server/workers.go:361-464. Persist the ExecuteLoopWorkerSpec as today, then start the current ddx binary as ddx work --server-managed <worker-id> or an equivalent hidden internal entry point in its own process group. Record nonzero PID and PGID on WorkerRecord and status.json. Keep the existing in-process path only where tests explicitly inject it, and do not make externally reported workers look server-owned.

NON-SCOPE
Do not kill external reported workers or interactive Claude/Codex sessions. Do not change bead claim semantics except releasing claims for the stopped/reaped managed worker. Do not implement UI controls.

GOVERNING ARTIFACT AND RELATIONSHIP

Parent bead ddx-c6317784 is too broad for one execution pass. This child owns part 2 of 5 of that parent scope.

ROOT CAUSE
# PROBLEM
The bead backend interface today (`cli/internal/bead/backend.go`) is JSONL-shaped: `RawBackend` requires whole-corpus `ReadAll`/`WriteAll`, and `Backend` declares only 22 of `*Store`'s 69 public methods. Per TD-027 (the canonical Bead Storage System and Lifecycle spec), the bead architecture needs:

- A typed `Operation` pattern with `Apply(b *Bead) error` (CAS via error returns)
- 11 LSP-clean storage sub-interfaces composing `Backend`
- Pluggable `IDGenerator` and parallel `LifecycleSubscriber`
- `context.Context` first-parameter discipline on every interface method
- Sentinel errors (`ErrNotFound`, `ErrConflict`, `ErrInvalidID`, `ErrAlreadyClaimed`, `ErrNotClaimedByOwner`, `ErrUnsupported`)
- The optional `OperationApplier` interface on `RawBackend` so JSONL backends can preserve heartbeat sidecar optimization
- A canonical Bead data model (field enumeration + invariants + JSONL wire format + schema-versioning policy)

This bead lays the foundation that all per-concern beads (L/C/E/X/Q/D/A/MIG) depend on. It declares the event reader/writer interfaces but deliberately does not migrate `AppendEvent`, `Events`, or `EventsByKind`; the E bead owns event-method ctx threading and `*Store` event interface satisfaction.

# ROOT CAUSE
- `cli/internal/bead/backend.go:10-67` — `RawBackend` (4 whole-corpus methods) + `Backend` (22 high-level methods, incomplete).
- `cli/internal/bead/store.go` — `*Store` exposes 69 public methods, none take `ctx context.Context`.
- `cli/internal/bead/axon_backend.go:340-381` — `WriteAll` ships entire corpus per mutation (catastrophic for Postgres).
- Operation pattern, IDGenerator, sentinel errors, context discipline — all absent.
- Bead data model (the canonical schema of fields + invariants) — only implicit in the Go struct.

# PROPOSED FIX
Implement TD-027 §5–§13 foundation pieces that are required before the per-concern beads can execute.

## New files

1. `cli/internal/bead/operation.go` — `Operation` interface with `Apply(b *Bead) error`. `MutateFunc` adapter for ad-hoc mutations. Empty named-op section (per-concern beads add their op types).
2. `cli/internal/bead/id.go` — `ValidateID`, format constants (`DefaultIDPrefix`, `MaxIDLength`, `MinIDLength`), `IDGenerator` interface, `RandomHexIDGenerator`, `SequentialIDGenerator`, `NewIDGenerator()` default.
3. `cli/internal/bead/errors.go` — sentinel e
...[truncated]

PROPOSED FIX
Implement only this child acceptance slice:
- Sentinel errors declared as distinct `error` values. Test: `TestSentinelErrors_AreErrorValues`.
- Context typed accessors implemented. Tests: `TestWithIdentity_RoundTrip`, `TestIdentityFromContext_AbsentReturnsFalse`, `TestWithTrace_RoundTrip`.
- Foundational sub-interfaces (`BeadInitializer`, `BeadReader`, `BeadLifecycle`, `BeadEventReader`, `BeadEventWriter`) declared with ctx-first signatures. `*Store` satisfies only the non-event foundation interfaces in this bead — compile-time assertions: `var _ BeadInitializer = (*Store)(nil)`, `var _ BeadReader = (*Store)(nil)`, `var _ BeadLifecycle = (*Store)(nil)`. No `BeadEventReader`/`BeadEventWriter` assertion is added until E.

.ddx/beads.jsonl:29:{"created_at":"2026-06-29T10:45:26.873700708Z","description":"PROBLEM
Observed on 2026-06-29 in this repo while draining the queue. Worker pid 4014720 hit 4 consecutive 'transport: exit status 1' outcomes within ~2h on different beads (ddx-9aa2151d, ddx-95d80f7e, ddx-1c865bdb, ddx-aef568e7) without any successful merge. Load average was 38-52 throughout (8-core host). After each transport failure the worker advances to the next ready bead and the cycle repeats; closed counter unchanged across the period.

Specific log signature:
  do response complete in <N>m
  tracker_lock section=durable_audit wait=1ms hold=<seconds>
  ✗ ddx-<id> → transport: exit status 1

Some attempts had very short responses (1m37s) suggesting codex died early; others completed long (32m+) then died at durable_audit. Common factor: the provider subprocess exits status 1, which DDx classifies as 'transport' (not tests_red, not no_changes, not timeout).

ROOT CAUSE WITH FILE:LINE
- The outcome classifier maps any non-zero provider exit (codex/claude/etc.) to 'transport: exit status 1' regardless of cause (OOM, kill, network error mid-stream). Inspect cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go around the outcome-mapping path for the codex/provider exit code (search 'transport: exit status'). The classifier does not distinguish 'load-killed' from 'genuine bug' from 'provider API error'.
- Under high system load (load avg > ~3-5x CPU count), the provider subprocess gets starved during the durable_audit / land phase (which is git+disk heavy), bumps into kernel OOM-killer or hits its own internal heartbeat/timeout, and exits non-zero. DDx then walks the queue trying the next bead which also dies, burning context+tokens with zero forward progress.
- There is no backoff/cooldown when transport failures cluster (e.g., 3+ in N minutes).

PROPOSED FIX
1. Add load-pressure detection: before each claim, sample /proc/loadavg and compare to runtime.NumCPU(); if 5-min loadavg > k * cores (k=2-3 reasonable), emit a structured backoff observation and sleep proportional to overload before claiming.
2. Add transport-failure cooldown: when N (≥3) 'transport: exit status 1' outcomes occur within the last M minutes, the worker should pause for a longer interval (e.g., 5-15 min) before next claim instead of immediately consuming another bead.
3. Capture and surface the provider subprocess's exit reason where possible: check WaitStatus for signal-killed (SIGKILL → OOM-likely, SIGTERM → graceful), and distinguish in the outcome string (e.g., 'transport: provider killed by SIGKILL (likely OOM)').

IN-SCOPE FILES
- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go (outcome classifier + claim-loop pacing)
- cli/cmd/work.go (worker pacing/backoff)
- cli/internal/workerstatus/ (if load sampling needs a place)

OUT-OF-SCOPE
- Do not change the queue ordering.
- Do not change the durable_audit commit flow itself.
- Do not implement a general-purpose host load manager.

AC
1. New helper LoadPressureSnapshot() in cli/internal/workerstatus or cli/internal/agent returning normalized loadavg/cores; unit test covers >threshold and <threshold paths.
2. Worker pre-claim path checks the snapshot; when pressure exceeds k (default 2.5), logs a structured 'load_pressure_backoff' observation and sleeps before claim. Unit test asserts the path is invoked with k configurable.
3. Worker tracks last N (≥3) 'transport: exit status 1' outcomes by wall-clock timestamp; when 3 occur within 10m, the next claim is delayed by an extended cooldown (>=5m). Unit test simulates 3 failures within window and asserts the delay.
4. Outcome classifier distinguishes signal-killed (SIGKILL/SIGTERM) provider exits in the outcome string. Unit test asserts the distinct outcomes.
5. cd cli && go test ./cmd/... ./internal/agent/... ./internal/workerstatus/... -count=1 green.

The store factory still treats Axon as an experimental special case and only enables it behind an environment gate. The relevant branch is cli/internal/bead/store.go:117-137, which falls back to JSONL unless DDX_AXON_EXPERIMENTAL is truthy, even when config has backend: axon. The checked-in sample config also does not document the bead_tracker backend block yet (.ddx/config.yaml:1-17).

ROOT CAUSE
cli/internal/bead/store.go:117-137 and .ddx/config.yaml:1-17 do not present Axon as a normal selectable backend. The config type already has a Backend field, but the factory still requires an env override and emits a warning instead of honoring configuration directly.

PROPOSED FIX
- Update the store factory so bead_tracker.backend=axon selects the Axon backend from config without requiring the experimental env var.
- Update the checked-in .ddx/config.yaml example to show the backend and Axon connection block.
- Keep the default backend unchanged when backend is unset.
- Preserve bd/br behavior for the existing external backend path.

NON-SCOPE
- GraphQL client generation.
- Axon backend CRUD internals.
- Subscription transport.","events":[{"actor":"ddx","body":"{"resolved_provider":"codex","resolved_model":"gpt-5.4-mini","fallback_chain":[],"actual_power":8}","created_at":"0001-01-01T00:00:00Z","kind":"routing","source":"ddx agent execute-bead","summary":"provider=codex model=gpt-5.4-mini"},{"actor":"ddx","body":"{"attempt_id":"20260506T092212-36d077c0","harness":"codex","model":"gpt-5.4-mini","input_tokens":3410571,"output_tokens":15610,"total_tokens":3426181,"cost_usd":0,"duration_ms":256113,"exit_code":0}","created_at":"0001-01-01T00:00:00Z","kind":"cost","source":"ddx agent execute-bead","summary":"tokens=3426181 model=gpt-5.4-mini"},{"actor":"ddx","body":"{"escalation_count":0,"fallback_chain":[],"final_tier":"","requested_profile":"","requested_tier":"","resolved_model":"gpt-5.4-mini","resolved_provider":"codex","resolved_tier":""}","created_at":"0001-01-01T00:00:00Z","kind":"routing","source":"ddx agent work","summary":"provider=codex model=gpt-5.4-mini"},{"actor":"erik","body":"failure_class=review-error: transport

Two competing hypotheses for why:
H1: ddx execution machinery (the 9 fail-prone layers) is unreliable — failure is in the workers/server code.
H2: Bead authoring quality is insufficient — beads punt investigation; hand-curation papers over the gap.

Both are plausible. Determining the relative contribution requires a 3-path study, NOT the original 2-path proposal.

THREE PATHS

Path A: ddx try / ddx work with the bead's auto-prompt verbatim (current production path).
Path B: Sub-agent dispatched with the bead's auto-prompt verbatim (NOT hand-curated). Same prompt as A; different executor. Isolates ddx machinery from prompt quality.
Path C: Sub-agent dispatched with operator hand-curated prompt (file:line cites, named tests, explicit non-scope, etc.). Establishes the ceiling.

INTERPRETATION
- A vs B differ → ddx machinery is the failure (file follow-ups against the 9 layers).
- B vs C differ → bead authoring quality is the failure (already covered by bead-quality-audit + retrofit).
- A == B << C → both are real, with bead quality being the dominant gap.

METHOD
Pick 5 representative CLOSED beads as fixtures: one trivial fix, one refactor child, one test addition, one multi-file feature, one documentation update. For each fixture, run all 3 paths on a branch from the bead's base-rev (parent of the original work commit). Throwaway commits — the original work is already in main. We're measuring path properties, not producing mergeable code.

MEASUREMENTS
Per path per bead:
- Time-to-first-claim (A) / time-to-first-output (B,C)
- Time-to-merge-eligible-commit (whether or not we merge)
- Attempts (A) / iterations (B,C)
- Status outcomes: success / no_changes / preserved / blocked / errored
- Cost: tokens, USD
- Manual interventions
- Failure mode classified per the 9-layer machinery list

OUTPUT
.ddx/executions/<run-id>/ddx-vs-subagent-comparison-2026-05-03.md with:
- Per-bead per-path table
- Aggregate stats (median + p99)
- Failure-mode distribution
- Hypothesis verdict (H1 vs H2 vs both)
- Recommendations for which path is appropriate for which bead-shape

TIMING
This study runs AFTER the executeloop refactor (C0-C13) lands so the ddx machinery is its post-refactor shape. Running pre-refactor measures behavior we're about to change. RELIABILITY-PRINCIPLES + bead-quality-audit + retrofit + refactor must all complete first. This bead is filed now and EXECUTION DEFERRED.

NOT IN SCOPE
- Fixing whatever the study reveals (separate bug beads filed from the study's findings)
- Running against pre-refactor ddx machinery (data we won't act on)
- Comparing different sub-agent harnesses (different concern)

EVIDENCE

PROBLEM
The two principles of epic ddx-602ae31a (ddx home resolution + skills/personas install topology) must behave identically across both modes (in-tree and convention). Today there is no single test suite that drives the same scenarios through both modes; coverage is per-mode-ad-hoc and divergence between modes is invisible until operators hit it in production.

ROOT CAUSE
The two parent beads (ddx-06cbaa90 and ddx-937de9fc) each carry per-feature tests. Neither owns the cross-mode equivalence guarantee.

PROPOSED FIX

1. Add cli/internal/agent/test_mode_matrix_test.go (or equivalent suite under a new package) that parameterizes every behaviour-equivalence scenario across both modes:

  for mode in [Convention, InTree]:
    setup_fixture(mode)

      "bead-lifecycle":           create/show/update/close
      "bead-execution":           ddx try a bead, observe attempt evidence and state mutation
      "plugin-skill-resolution":  ddx-side plugin lookup honors precedence; agent-tier symlinks resolve to plugin content
      "persona-binding":          role → persona resolves correctly per binding config
      "worker-drain":             ddx work --watch picks beads and lands them

      "attachments":              per-attempt event files written to the right anchor
      "index-lock-recovery":      gitlock recovery fires correctly in both modes
    ]:
      run scenario in current mode
      assert behaviour identical to the other mode modulo storage path

2. Fixture setup must be deterministic and isolated:
   - Convention mode fixture: tempdir as project root, no .ddx/; XDG_DATA_HOME pointed at a per-test tempdir; git remote set to a deterministic URL.
   - In-tree mode fixture: tempdir as project root WITH .ddx/ pre-created; XDG_DATA_HOME pointed elsewhere (must not be consulted).
   - Two clones of the same project share an XDG dir — explicitly tested as a separate sub-scenario.

3. Document the test-mode invariant in cli/internal/agent/test_mode_matrix_test.go's package doc: "Any behavior that diverges between Convention and InTree modes (other than the on-disk anchor) is a regression."

  The execute-bead subsystem files use execute_bead_* naming that conflates the orchestrator, the attempt, the review, and the loop under one prefix, making package boundaries and import graphs ambiguous. The refactor proposal was stored at /tmp/ and is unreadable post-session.

ROOT CAUSE
  cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go (drain loop), cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go (attempt), cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_orchestrator.go (land coordinator), cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_land.go (landing), cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_review.go (review), cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_review_triage.go (review triage), cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_status.go (disposition), and cli/internal/agent/executions_mirror.go (mirror) all share a flat package with unrelated concerns. The ddx try CLI command named in FEAT-010 does not exist (cli/cmd/try.go absent).

PROPOSED FIX
  Rename per the following table (no behavior change):
    cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go             -> cli/internal/agent/try/attempt.go (already exists; merge or verify)
    cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_orchestrator.go -> cli/internal/agent/try/land.go
    cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_land.go         -> cli/internal/agent/try/coordinator.go
    cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_review.go       -> cli/internal/agent/try/review.go
    cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_review_triage.go -> cli/internal/agent/try/review_triage.go
    cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_status.go       -> cli/internal/agent/try/disposition.go
    cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go         -> cli/internal/agent/work/drain.go
    cli/internal/agent/executions_mirror.go         -> cli/internal/agent/try/mirror.go

  Rename identifiers: ExecuteBeadWorker -> work.Worker, ExecuteBeadWorker.Run -> work.Worker.Drain, ExecuteBead -> try.Attempt (FEAT-006 + FEAT-010).

  Add cli/cmd/try.go with cobra command "ddx try <bead-id>" invoking try.Attempt directly (FEAT-010 surface).

  Update FEAT-006 and FEAT-010 spec docs to reflect new package paths. Keep ExecuteBead* shims as typedefs; deletion is C14.

NON-SCOPE
  - Behavior changes to the drain loop or attempt logic.
  - Deletion of ExecuteBead* typedefs (C14).
  - ddx-dc157075 file-path reference migration if that bead has not yet landed.

INTERSECTIONS
- P2: file and identifier names should reflect the subsystem boundary and keep responsibilities separated.
- P5: ddx try as a first-class CLI surface makes the worker/attempt state easier for operators to see.

Existing land/divergence tests still pass. 3. cd cli && go test ./internal/agent/... passes; go build ./... succeeds.","created_at":"2026-06-28T03:08:04.1203069Z","description":"PROBLEM
Agents keep tripping the execute-bead one-commit/no-amend contract (the implementation commit is rewritten/amended/rebased after the first commit). Today this is DETECTED post-hoc and the whole attempt is discarded and re-run, wasting long, expensive runs (observed 3047s / 15.3M-token attempts thrown away).

ROOT CAUSE WITH FILE:LINE
- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_land.go (divergence/ancestry resolution, ~line 187 and ~910-927): a diverged/rewritten candidate is resolved by aborting the claim (detect-then-fail), not by preventing or correcting the rewrite.

PROPOSED FIX
- Prevent or auto-correct the contract violation rather than discarding the attempt: e.g. detect a post-first-commit rewrite before land and reconcile it (or instruct/guard the harness to not amend) so a successful candidate is preserved instead of re-run from scratch.
- This is DISTINCT from the mixed_commit_and_no_changes_rationale detector in execute_bead.go:~1419 (commit+rationale mismatch). Do NOT target that path.

NON-SCOPE
- Do not relax the audit-trail rule (never squash/rebase/filter landed attempt history).
- Do not touch the mixed_commit detector.
- Do not change Fizeau routing.

PROVENANCE
Derived from a session-harvest of local Claude/Codex operator prompts (2026-06-27), classified into deficiency-papering vs intent, then put through two adversarial-review rounds that dropped every item already shipped. This item survived as a verified, non-redundant gap. See docs/session-harvest-playbook.md.","id":"ddx-3c865b16","issue_type":"task","labels":["area:agent","area:execute-bead","kind:reliability","source:session-harvest-2026-06-27"],"priority":2,"schema_version":1,"status":"open","title":"Make execute-bead auto-comply with the one-commit/no-amend contract","updated_at":"2026-06-28T03:08:04.1203069Z"}

The public DDx execution vocabulary is supposed to be `ddx run`, `ddx try`, and `ddx work`, but large parts of the codebase still use legacy `execute-bead` / `work` names in public help, comments, server worker kinds, GraphQL models, tests, docs, tracker metadata helpers, and internal symbols. Operators now see mixed terms and developers keep adding new code under the old names.

ROOT CAUSE WITH FILE:LINE
- cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1485-1563 still defines the legacy `ddx agent work` cobra surface as if it were primary: help says "work is the primary queue-driven execution surface", examples use `ddx agent work`, and deprecation text still names work rather than pointing to `ddx work`.
- cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1590-1940 keeps the main work implementation named `runAgentExecuteLoopImpl`, `writeExecuteLoopResult`, and `ExecuteBeadLoopResult`, so new work-path changes naturally copy the old terminology.
- cli/cmd/try.go:213-333 implements the new `ddx try` command by constructing `ExecuteBeadExecutor`, `ExecuteBeadRuntime`, `ExecuteBeadWorker`, and `ExecuteBeadLoopRuntime`, so the new public command still routes through legacy names.
- cli/internal/server/graphql/models.go:2233-2281 and cli/internal/server/frontend/e2e/workers.spec.ts:343-397 expose worker kind/comment strings as `work` / `execute-bead`, which leaks legacy naming through API and UI fixtures.
- docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-031-bead-state-machine.md:215-262 still describes attempt results and retry metadata as `execute-bead` / `work-*`, even while FEAT-010 and ADR-023 name the lifecycle `try` / `work`.
- Existing related beads are partially stale or narrower than this repo-wide cleanup: ddx-387a0178 owns C13 file/identifier renames and still says `ddx try` does not exist, while ddx-29058e2a / ddx-1a9cc01f still propose `ExecuteLoopSpec` naming for a work-side spec.

WHY THE NAMES DIVERGED
The codebase grew from the historical `ddx agent execute-bead` and `ddx agent work` commands. FEAT-010 introduced the clearer layered vocabulary (`run` = invocation atom, `try` = one bead attempt, `work` = queue drain), and `ddx try` / `ddx work` now exist as the intended operator surface. The implementation retained old symbols and compatibility aliases, so old names persisted as the easiest local pattern to copy.

PROPOSED FIX
- Perform a repo-wide terminology audit using `rg -n "work|ExecuteLoop|execute loop|execute-bead|ExecuteBead|execute bead"` and classify each hit as: public/user-facing rename, internal compatibility alias to retain, durable metadata key to retain with documentation, historical docs intentionally retained, or generated fixture/test data.
- Update public/user-facing CLI help, comments, API/GraphQL descriptions, server/frontend labels, tests, and docs to prefer `work` and `try`.
- Rename non-compatibility internal types/functions introduced by the FEAT-010 path from ExecuteLoop/ExecuteBead vocabulary to Work/Try vocabulary. Coordinate with ddx-387a0178 C13; if C13 already performs a rename, fold this cleanup into or after it rather than duplicating work.
- For compatibility aliases and durable data keys that must remain (`ddx agent work`, `ddx agent execute-bead`, `work-*` Extra keys, historical event kinds), add concise comments documenting them as legacy/back-compat names and point readers at `ddx work` / `ddx try`.
- Re-scope or update related open beads whose titles/descriptions still establish new `ExecuteLoop*` names (notably ddx-29058e2a and ddx-1a9cc01f) so future work does not add fresh legacy terminology.

NON-SCOPE
- Do not remove the legacy `ddx agent work` or `ddx agent execute-bead` command aliases in this bead; they are compatibility surfaces unless a governing doc explicitly authorizes removal.
- Do not rename durable tracker keys or historical event kinds if doing so would break existing `.ddx/beads.jsonl`, `.ddx/executions`, worker records, or archive reads. Document retained keys instead.
- Do not change work/try behavior, queue ordering, claim semantics, routing policy, or Fizeau integration.
- Do not hand-edit `.ddx/beads.jsonl`; update any related bead metadata only with `ddx bead update` / `ddx bead dep`.

INTERSECTIONS
- ddx-387a0178: structural C13 file/identifier rename; this bead depends on it or should be folded into it if it is re-authored.
- ddx-29058e2a and ddx-1a9cc01f: ExecuteLoopSpec naming conflicts with the intended WorkSpec/WorkRequest vocabulary; update or supersede as part of this cleanup.
- ddx-eccc6efb: server-spawn migration may delete some `ExecuteLoopWorkerSpec` paths; if it lands first, shrink this bead to remaining public/API/doc names.","id":"ddx-3ee4ddcf","issue_type":"task","labels":["area:agent","area:docs","area:server","kind:refactor","nomenclature","phase:2","prevention","spec:FEAT-010"],"notes":"Superseded by ddx-f088a3fd / ddx-b194d20f and audited by ddx-bdc0065e. This plan retains ddx agent compatibility aliases and legacy cli/internal/agent package targets, so it must not dispatch.","parent":"ddx-5cb6e6cd","priority":4,"schema_version":1,"spec-id":"FEAT-010","status":"cancelled","title":"agent: replace legacy execute terminology with work/try nomenclature","updated_at":"2026-05-15T21:27:47.264393651Z"}

The two principles of epic ddx-602ae31a (ddx home resolution + skills/personas install topology) must behave identically across both modes (in-tree and convention). Today there is no single test suite that drives the same scenarios through both modes; coverage is per-mode-ad-hoc and divergence between modes is invisible until operators hit it in production.

ROOT CAUSE
The two parent beads (ddx-06cbaa90 and ddx-937de9fc) each carry per-feature tests. Neither owns the cross-mode equivalence guarantee.

PROPOSED FIX

1. Add cli/internal/agent/test_mode_matrix_test.go (or equivalent suite under a new package) that parameterizes every behaviour-equivalence scenario across both modes:

  for mode in [Convention, InTree]:
    setup_fixture(mode)
    for scenario in [
      "bead-lifecycle":           create/show/update/close
      "bead-execution":           ddx try a bead, observe attempt evidence and state mutation
      "plugin-skill-resolution":  ddx-side plugin lookup honors precedence; agent-tier symlinks resolve to plugin content
      "persona-binding":          role → persona resolves correctly per binding config
      "worker-drain":             ddx work --watch picks beads and lands them
      "meta-prompt-sync":         CLAUDE.md (or equivalent) gets the right content
      "auto-commit-routing":      bead create/update auto-commit lands in the right git repo (project's vs XDG's)
      "attachments":              per-attempt event files written to the right anchor
      "index-lock-recovery":      gitlock recovery fires correctly in both modes
    ]:
      run scenario in current mode
      assert behaviour identical to the other mode modulo storage path

2. Fixture setup must be deterministic and isolated:
   - Convention mode fixture: tempdir as project root, no .ddx/; XDG_DATA_HOME pointed at a per-test tempdir; git remote set to a deterministic URL.
   - In-tree mode fixture: tempdir as project root WITH .ddx/ pre-created; XDG_DATA_HOME pointed elsewhere (must not be consulted).
   - Two clones of the same project share an XDG dir — explicitly tested as a separate sub-scenario.

3. Document the test-mode invariant in cli/internal/agent/test_mode_matrix_test.go's package doc: "Any behavior that diverges between Convention and InTree modes (other than the on-disk anchor) is a regression."

4. SPEC UPDATES — propagate the resolver/mode model to every doc that references .ddx/ paths or project state. Each spec update added in this bead's commit cites principle-numbered tags so future readers see the link:
   - TD-031 bead state machine — "Storage anchors via ddxroot.Path(); see ddx-06cbaa90."
   - SD-019 multi-project-server-topology — Project identity from `host/owner/repo`; reference ddx-06cbaa90.
   - SD-020 multi-machine-coordinator-topology — same.
   - ADR-021 operator-prompt-beads-web-write-path — reference ddx-06cbaa90.
   - workers-sessions-operator-surface.md — worker status paths through ddxroot.Path().
   - plan-2026-04-29-artifact-and-run-architecture — FEAT-010 paths through resolver.
   - library/skills/ddx/SKILL.md and CLAUDE.md — install precedence per ddx-937de9fc.
   - library/README.md — install topology overview.

NON-SCOPE
- New behaviours (every scenario in the matrix is already covered by ddx-06cbaa90 or ddx-937de9fc per-feature tests; this is the cross-mode equivalence layer).
- Migration tooling for existing in-tree repos (out of scope; this repo stays in-tree).

DEPS

PROBLEM
Parent bead ddx-c6317784 is too broad for one execution pass. This child owns part 2 of 5 of that parent scope.

ROOT CAUSE
# PROBLEM
The bead backend interface today (`cli/internal/bead/backend.go`) is JSONL-shaped: `RawBackend` requires whole-corpus `ReadAll`/`WriteAll`, and `Backend` declares only 22 of `*Store`'s 69 public methods. Per TD-027 (the canonical Bead Storage System and Lifecycle spec), the bead architecture needs:

- A typed `Operation` pattern with `Apply(b *Bead) error` (CAS via error returns)
- 11 LSP-clean storage sub-interfaces composing `Backend`
- Pluggable `IDGenerator` and parallel `LifecycleSubscriber`
- `context.Context` first-parameter discipline on every interface method
- Sentinel errors (`ErrNotFound`, `ErrConflict`, `ErrInvalidID`, `ErrAlreadyClaimed`, `ErrNotClaimedByOwner`, `ErrUnsupported`)
- The optional `OperationApplier` interface on `RawBackend` so JSONL backends can preserve heartbeat sidecar optimization
- A canonical Bead data model (field enumeration + invariants + JSONL wire format + schema-versioning policy)

This bead lays the foundation that all per-concern beads (L/C/E/X/Q/D/A/MIG) depend on. It declares the event reader/writer interfaces but deliberately does not migrate `AppendEvent`, `Events`, or `EventsByKind`; the E bead owns event-method ctx threading and `*Store` event interface satisfaction.

# ROOT CAUSE
- `cli/internal/bead/backend.go:10-67` — `RawBackend` (4 whole-corpus methods) + `Backend` (22 high-level methods, incomplete).
- `cli/internal/bead/store.go` — `*Store` exposes 69 public methods, none take `ctx context.Context`.
- `cli/internal/bead/axon_backend.go:340-381` — `WriteAll` ships entire corpus per mutation (catastrophic for Postgres).
- Operation pattern, IDGenerator, sentinel errors, context discipline — all absent.
- Bead data model (the canonical schema of fields + invariants) — only implicit in the Go struct.

# PROPOSED FIX
Implement TD-027 §5–§13 foundation pieces that are required before the per-concern beads can execute.

## New files

1. `cli/internal/bead/operation.go` — `Operation` interface with `Apply(b *Bead) error`. `MutateFunc` adapter for ad-hoc mutations. Empty named-op section (per-concern beads add their op types).
2. `cli/internal/bead/id.go` — `ValidateID`, format constants (`DefaultIDPrefix`, `MaxIDLength`, `MinIDLength`), `IDGenerator` int
...[truncated]

PROPOSED FIX
Implement only this child acceptance slice:
- Context typed accessors implemented. Tests: `TestWithIdentity_RoundTrip`, `TestIdentityFromContext_AbsentReturnsFalse`, `TestWithTrace_RoundTrip`.
- Foundational sub-interfaces (`BeadInitializer`, `BeadReader`, `BeadLifecycle`, `BeadEventReader`, `BeadEventWriter`) declared with ctx-first signatures. `*Store` satisfies only the non-event foundation interfaces in this bead — compile-time assertions: `var _ BeadInitializer = (*Store)(nil)`, `var _ BeadReader = (*Store)(nil)`, `var _ BeadLifecycle = (*Store)(nil)`. No `BeadEventReader`/`BeadEventWriter` assertion is added until E.

Parent bead ddx-eea2dfca is too broad for one execution pass. This child owns part 3 of 3 of that parent scope.

ROOT CAUSE
PROBLEM
A Niflheim worker repeatedly emitted pre-claim readiness/intake warnings for the same systemic dirty/generated DDx artifact condition and continued polling/releasing. That looks like forward progress but does not create a clear operator-visible system-unready lane after the configured threshold.

ROOT CAUSE WITH FILE:LINE
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go:1967-2072` emits pre-claim timeout/system-unready outcomes as warn-only by default and continues implementation when strict intake blocking is off.
- `cli/internal/agent/failclass/failclass.go:103-142` says setup/dirty worktree/system outcomes should not escalate, but repeated pre-claim warnings are not consistently classified into retry/operator-attention decisions before worker retry loops continue.

PROPOSED FIX
Add a self-contained Niflheim-style pre-claim fixture or table-driven case under `cli/internal/agent/...` for repeated readiness timeout/system-unready warnings with generated DDx artifact dirt such as `.ddx/harness-sessions/...json`. After the configured same-fingerprint threshold, classify the condition as system-unready/operator-attention and release/park without implementation retry or model escalation. Align with `ddx-d67f124e` rather than duplicating its lower-level implementation.

NON-SCOPE
- Do not change Fizeau route selection or hard-code concrete provider/model names.
- Do not edit Niflheim beads or Niflheim implementation code.
- Do not duplicate the low-level index-contention fix tracked by `ddx-2965925e`.
- Do not duplicate the post-commit review/land timeout work tracked by `ddx-b2846610`.
- Do not duplicate the repeated pre-claim warn operator-attention bead `ddx-d67f124e`; this bead only adds the Niflheim observation as regression coverage and decision/audit wiring.
- Do not implement a full operator-lane product workflow beyond state transitions/events needed to keep the worker from blind retry or false close.

INTERSECTIONS
Parent: `ddx-3106674f`. Dependencies to preserve: `ddx-2965925e`, `ddx-b2846610`, `ddx-d67f124e`. Related: `ddx-e1a576a7`, `ddx-a9d130d0`, `ddx-ac10e7af`. Governing references: FEAT-010, TP-021, ADR-024, docs/dev/engineering-principles.md:39-45.

PROPOSED FIX
Implement only this child acceptance slice:
- `cd cli && go test ./internal/agent/... ./cmd/... -run 'NiflheimEvidence|PreClaim|RetryEscalation|PreClaimDecisionAudit' -count=1` passes.
- `cd cli && go test ./internal/agent/... ./cmd/... -count=1` passes.
- `lefthook run pre-commit` passes.

NON-SCOPE
Do not implement parent acceptance criteria assigned to sibling child beads. (2) The typeface (`Inter`, tailwind.config.js:39-46) is SPECIFIED but never LOADED (no @font-face/@fontsource/Google Fonts anywhere) -> renders the OS system-sans fallback.

ROOT CAUSE: the design:export pipeline emits the generic default token DATA (not DESIGN.md's warm palette); and no font asset is imported. Per codex: `semanticColors` is the load-bearing alias layer (KEEP it) — only the generated token DATA is stale. Add Inter via `@fontsource-variable/inter` (`bun add`), import in `cli/internal/server/frontend/src/app.css`, so all non-mono roles render Inter instead of system fallback.

2. TestModeMatrix_OperatorExpectationDocumented asserts the suite's package godoc clearly states the cross-mode equivalence invariant.","created_at":"2026-07-02T18:52:15.477090595Z","description":"PROBLEM
Parent bead ddx-43b6b5fe is too broad for one execution pass. This child owns part 2 of 4 of that parent scope.

ROOT CAUSE
PROBLEM
The two principles of epic ddx-602ae31a (ddx home resolution + skills/personas install topology) must behave identically across both modes (in-tree and convention). Today there is no single test suite that drives the same scenarios through both modes; coverage is per-mode-ad-hoc and divergence between modes is invisible until operators hit it in production.

ROOT CAUSE
The two parent beads (ddx-06cbaa90 and ddx-937de9fc) each carry per-feature tests. Neither owns the cross-mode equivalence guarantee.

PROPOSED FIX

1. Add cli/internal/agent/test_mode_matrix_test.go (or equivalent suite under a new package) that parameterizes every behaviour-equivalence scenario across both modes:

  for mode in [Convention, InTree]:
    setup_fixture(mode)
    for scenario in [
      "bead-lifecycle":           create/show/update/close
      "bead-execution":           ddx try a bead, observe attempt evidence and state mutation
      "plugin-skill-resolution":  ddx-side plugin lookup honors precedence; agent-tier symlinks resolve to plugin content
      "persona-binding":          role → persona resolves correctly per binding config
      "worker-drain":             ddx work --watch picks beads and lands them
      "meta-prompt-sync":         CLAUDE.md (or equivalent) gets the right content
      "auto-commit-routing":      bead create/update auto-commit lands in the right git repo (project's vs XDG's)
      "attachments":              per-attempt event files written to the right anchor
      "index-lock-recovery":      gitlock recovery fires correctly in both modes
    ]:
      run scenario in current mode
      assert behaviour identical to the other mode modulo storage path

2. Fixture setup must be deterministic and isolated:
   - Convention mode fixture: tempdir as project root, no .ddx/; XDG_DATA_HOME pointed at a per-test tempdir; git remote set to a deterministic URL.
   - In-tree mode fixture: tempdir as project root WITH .ddx/ pre-created; XDG_DATA_HOME pointed elsewhere (must not be consulted).
   - Two clones of the same project share an XDG dir — explicitly tested as a separate sub-scenario.

3. Document the test-mode invariant in cli/internal/agent/test_mode_matrix_test.go's package doc: "Any behavior that diverges between Convention and InTree modes (other than the on-disk anchor) is a regression."

4. SPEC UPDATES — propagate the resolver/mode m
...[truncated]

PROPOSED FIX
Implement only this child acceptance slice:
- TestModeMatrix_TwoClonesShareXDG asserts two project worktrees pointing at the same git remote share the XDG project dir AND register both in worktrees.json with one auto-pinned as master.
- TestModeMatrix_OperatorExpectationDocumented asserts the suite's package godoc clearly states the cross-mode equivalence invariant.

NON-SCOPE
Do not implement parent acceptance criteria assigned to sibling child beads.
